comments_and_signatures.comments:
I am applying for assistance through the 2022 Member Assistance Program due to a significant and unexpected reduction in my household income over the past year, coupled with increased caregiving responsibilities and ongoing student loan obligations.

I am a board-certified addiction psychiatrist in my sixth year of practice, currently working full-time at Riverside Community Behavioral Health Center, a safety-net clinic that primarily serves uninsured and underinsured patients in Denver, Colorado. Approximately 70% of my caseload consists of patients with co-occurring substance use and serious mental illness, many of whom are experiencing homelessness or unstable housing, legal system involvement, or recent incarceration.

Over the past two years, our clinic has seen a dramatic increase in opioid- and methamphetamine-related presentations, as well as alcohol use disorders that worsened during the COVID-19 pandemic. I have been leading a small interdisciplinary team focused on expanding access to medications for opioid use disorder (MOUD), including buprenorphine and extended-release naltrexone. We have also been piloting group-based relapse prevention programming in both English and Spanish, in collaboration with our peer specialists and social workers.

Financially, my situation has become more challenging since mid-2021. My partner was laid off from his job in the hospitality industry, and although he has been doing intermittent contract work, his income is highly inconsistent. We also became legal guardians for my younger brother, who has a chronic medical condition and recently moved in with us after our mother’s health declined. As a result, our household expenses have increased substantially (medications, transportation to appointments, and therapy costs for my brother), while our overall income has decreased.

At the same time, my fixed expenses remain high. I am still repaying medical school loans and a small personal loan I took out during residency when I briefly had to reduce my hours due to a health issue. While I have tried to prioritize professional dues and continuing education, this year I am at a point where I must make difficult decisions between essential household expenses and professional development costs, including membership and meeting attendance.

AAAP has been my primary professional home since fellowship. I previously attended the 2018 and 2019 annual meetings and one regional meeting in 2021, which were tremendously valuable for my clinical practice. For example, after attending a workshop on integrating harm reduction approaches into outpatient psychiatry, I helped develop a brief naloxone education protocol and overdose prevention counseling workflow at our clinic. Our team has distributed naloxone kits to more than 200 patients and family members in the past year, and we have multiple documented instances in which patients or their loved ones used these kits to reverse overdoses in the community.

I am requesting assistance with both annual dues and partial coverage of the registration, airfare, and lodging costs to attend the 2022 AAAP Annual Meeting. I am particularly interested in sessions addressing stimulant use disorders, equity in addiction treatment, and practical strategies for integrating trauma-informed care in community mental health settings. I plan to bring back concrete tools, handouts, and protocols to share with our clinic’s multidisciplinary team. Historically, I have given in-service presentations following AAAP meetings, and I intend to do the same this year, focusing on updates in pharmacologic and psychosocial treatments for opioid and stimulant use disorders.

In terms of my commitment to AAAP, I have previously participated in the mentoring program as a mentee and have recently started informally mentoring two early-career colleagues in our local community who are interested in addiction psychiatry. In the future, I hope to become more involved in AAAP committees related to community and public sector practice, health equity, and education. Continued membership and meeting attendance are important for building those relationships and staying current with evidence-based practices.

Given our current financial strain, without assistance through the MAP program I will likely be unable to renew my membership or attend the annual meeting this year. I have explored support from my institution; however, our continuing education and travel budgets were significantly reduced during the pandemic, and our medical director has confirmed that they are unable to provide financial assistance for conference travel or membership dues this fiscal year. I am therefore turning to the MAP program as a last resort to maintain my active engagement with AAAP and to continue to bring high-quality, up-to-date addiction treatment practices back to a highly underserved patient population.

Thank you for considering my application and for the work you do to support members who are working in resource-limited settings and navigating financial hardship. I am grateful for the opportunities AAAP has already provided me and hope to remain an active member of this community.
